input.query_title: Random graphs with arbitrary degree distributions and their applications
input.query_abstract: Recent work on the structure of social networks and the internet has focussed attention on graphs with distributions of vertex degree that are significantly different from the Poisson degree distributions that have been widely studied in the past. In this paper we develop in detail the theory of random graphs with arbitrary degree distributions. In addition to simple undirected, unipartite graphs, we examine the properties of directed and bipartite graphs. Among other results, we derive exact expressions for the position of the phase transition at which a giant component first forms, the mean component size, the size of the giant component if there is one, the mean number of vertices a certain distance away from a randomly chosen vertex, and the average vertex-vertex distance within a graph. We apply our theory to some real-world graphs, including the world-wide web and collaboration graphs of scientists and Fortune 1000 company directors. We demonstrate that in some cases random graphs with appropriate distributions of vertex degree predict with surprising accuracy the behavior of the real world, while in others there is a measurable discrepancy between theory and reality, perhaps indicating the presence of additional social structure in the network that is not captured by the random graph.
input.hyp_generic_object: aftershock cascades in which each event triggers an arbitrary number of subsequent events (arbitrary triggering-productivity distribution)
input.hyp_abstract: Whether a mainshock initiates a self-limiting aftershock sequence or a prolonged, cascading swarm depends on how many subsequent events each shock triggers, a quantity that varies strongly with magnitude and cannot be captured by its average alone. We treat triggered seismicity as a branching process in which every event spawns a random number of direct offspring events drawn from an arbitrary productivity distribution, encoded through a probability generating function. Assuming that triggering lineages are locally treelike, each aftershock attributable to a single parent, self-consistent composition of the generating function yields the critical branching ratio separating sequences that decay to background from those with finite probability of runaway proliferation. We obtain closed-form expressions for the probability that a triggering event founds a large sequence, the expected total number of events in a decaying sequence, and the mean count of descendants within a given number of triggering generations, each as derivatives of the generating function at unit argument. The framework accommodates depth- and magnitude-dependent productivity and multiple source regions with distinct triggering spectra. Fitted to regional catalogs, the theory reproduces observed sequence-size and duration statistics accurately in some tectonic settings, whereas systematic overdispersion elsewhere signals spatial clustering of triggering not represented by the independent-lineage assumption.
input.candidate_title: Birth, survival and death of languages by Monte Carlo simulation
input.candidate_abstract: Simulations of physicists for the competition between adult languages since 2003 are reviewed. How many languages are spoken by how many people? How many languages are contained in various language families? How do language similarities decay with geographical distance, and what effects do natural boundaries have? New simulations of bilinguality are given in an appendix.
